text:
If you work in an office or any kind of business setting, you've probably heard people mention a memo. But what is a memo, exactly? Is it just another kind of work email, or does it serve a different purpose? How are memos used—and more importantly, how do you actually write one?

In this article, we'll walk through the basics of business memos: what they are, when to use them, and how to write them so people actually understand and act on them. You'll also get some practical tips to make your memos clear, professional, and easy to read.

What is a business memo?

The word memo comes from memorandum. In everyday life, some people use "memo" to mean any quick note. In a business context, though, a memo is a short, formal message used to share information inside a company or organization.

What kind of information shows up in a business memo? That depends on the company, but you'll often see things like:

Announcements about meetings, new rules, schedule changes, or updates to normal routines

Budget or financial details, such as costs, targets, or spending changes

Confirmations of things that were already discussed in a meeting or call

Explanations of workplace problems and instructions on how to handle them

Requests for information, feedback, or specific actions from staff

Project updates, sales summaries, or other recent performance reports

Guidelines on health, safety, and other workplace procedures

Invitations or information about charity events or community involvement

Is a business memo the same as a work email?

A business memo isn't quite the same as a casual work email. Memos are usually more formal and structured, and they're often sent to a whole team or department instead of just one person.

Most companies send memos electronically now, which is why they can seem similar to emails. However, some workplaces still print memos and post them on bulletin boards or hand them out so everyone can see them easily.

How do you write a business memo?

If someone has asked you to write a memo and you're not sure how to begin, don't worry. You can follow a simple series of steps to organize your message, choose the right tone, and make sure your memo is clear and useful for the people who read it.
